own_review.issue: The new bounded ThreadPoolExecutor (core 0, max 4096, SynchronousQueue, default AbortPolicy) throws RejectedExecutionException from threadPool.execute() when all 4096 workers are busy. run() catches only AsynchronousCloseException and IOException; REE is a RuntimeException, so it escapes run(), kills the LocalServer accept thread, and the server permanently stops accepting connections. Worse, isAlive() returns listener.isOpen() (line 193-196) which stays true, so health checks never detect the dead accept loop. Fix: catch RejectedExecutionException inside the loop (close socket, log, continue) or use CallerRunsPolicy/bounded LinkedBlockingQueue.
When execute() rejects a task, the just-accepted clientSocket is never closed: file-descriptor leak and a remote client hung waiting forever. Any rejection-handling fix must close the channel.

The diff adds 'import static com.qubole.rubix.spi.CacheConfig.getServerMaxThreads' but never uses it (only getLocalTransferServerMaxThreads is called). The project's checkstyle config (src/checkstyle/checks.xml line 164, UnusedImports module, default error severity) runs in the maven validate phase, so this is a build-breaking violation.

Changing DEFAULT_SERVER_MAX_THREADS from Integer.MAX_VALUE to 4096 also rebounds BookKeeperServer's Thrift maxWorkerThreads (BookKeeperServer.java:141) — intended per the PR title but undocumented; and the new getLocalTransferServerMaxThreads() reuses that same constant as its default, coupling two semantically distinct settings.
New key 'rubix.local.transfer.max-threads' is inconsistent with the sibling key 'rubix.network.local.transfer.server.port' (KEY_LOCAL_SERVER_PORT) naming convention.

Non-issues verified: '%s' name format works fine with ThreadFactoryBuilder's integer counter; threads remain non-daemon exactly as with the old cached pool; core 0 + 60s keepAlive churn was explicitly accepted by the author in review ('thread creation is fast... keeping at 1m for simplicity').

points.point: Thread pool never shut down / 'resource leak, 4096 workers persist indefinitely after stop'
points.note: The 'never shut down' part is true, but the central consequence is false: with corePoolSize=0 and 60s keepAlive, idle workers terminate on their own; nothing persists indefinitely, and the behavior is identical to the pre-existing cached pool (also never shut down). Flags a non-regression with a fabricated consequence.

points.point: SynchronousQueue + AbortPolicy causes rejection under load; REE 'swallowed by catch (IOException) and never logged', connections 'silently fail'; suggests bounded queue or CallerRunsPolicy
points.note: The defect (rejection at saturation) is real and the proposed fixes are correct, so +1 — but the stated mechanism is verifiably false: RejectedExecutionException is a RuntimeException, is NOT caught by catch(IOException), and crashes the accept loop entirely. v4 downgrades a server-killing bug to a logging nit, so no critical credit.

points.point: DEFAULT_SERVER_MAX_THREADS change silently affects BookKeeperServer via getServerMaxThreads (BookKeeperServer.java:141)
points.note: Verified accurate, including the line reference; legitimate request to document the behavioral change.

points.point: getLocalTransferServerMaxThreads reuses DEFAULT_SERVER_MAX_THREADS, coupling two configs
points.note: Real but a minor design nit, largely duplicating the previous point's territory.

points.point: corePoolSize=0 means constant thread churn; suggest small core size
points.note: Matches the one human reference point — the author explicitly discussed and defended the keepAlive/churn tradeoff ('thread creation is fast... keeping at 1m for simplicity').

missed: Unused static import getServerMaxThreads in LocalDataTransferServer.java — checkstyle UnusedImports (checks.xml:164) runs at validate phase and breaks the build
exceeds: BookKeeperServer default-change impact (verified, beyond human reference)
Rejection-at-saturation concern (beyond human reference, though mechanism mis-stated)

points.point: threadPool local to run(), never shut down; workers live until 60s keep-alive expires
points.note: Accurately stated (unlike v4's version), but it is pre-existing behavior unchanged by this PR and self-healing via keepAlive; hygiene suggestion only.

points.point: SynchronousQueue + maxThreads 4096 + default AbortPolicy: REE is a RuntimeException not caught by the catch(AsynchronousCloseException)/catch(IOException) blocks — the accept loop will crash; suggests RejectedExecutionHandler or explicit catch
points.note: Exactly the key real defect on this PR, with the correct exception-propagation analysis and correct fix. Verified against run() at base commit.

points.point: DEFAULT_SERVER_MAX_THREADS MAX_VALUE->4096 affects both getServerMaxThreads (BookKeeperServer.java:141 Thrift maxWorkerThreads) and the new getter; silent behavioral change for existing deployments, asks for changelog note
points.note: Verified accurate, including the BookKeeperServer line reference.

points.point: Praise: separate config key allows independent tuning
threadsNamed Javadoc / ThreadFactoryBuilder sequential counter note
points.note: Self-answered non-issue; correct ('%s' with the integer counter works) but adds nothing actionable.

missed: Unused static import getServerMaxThreads in LocalDataTransferServer.java — checkstyle UnusedImports (checks.xml:164) runs at validate phase and breaks the build
Human reference point: the corePoolSize=0 / 60s keepAlive thread-churn tradeoff discussed at LocalDataTransferServer.java:184 (v4 raised it; v5 did not)
exceeds: RejectedExecutionException kills the accept loop — critical, correct, and beyond the human reference
BookKeeperServer default-change impact (verified, beyond human reference)

qualitative: v5 is the clearly more accurate review on the defect that matters: it correctly diagnoses that RejectedExecutionException from the bounded SynchronousQueue pool is a RuntimeException that escapes run()'s catch(AsynchronousCloseException)/catch(IOException) blocks and crashes the accept loop — the key real bug introduced by this PR. v4 spots the same rejection scenario but fabricates the mechanism, claiming the exception is 'swallowed by the outer catch (IOException)' and that connections merely 'silently fail', which inverts the severity; it also fabricates an 'indefinite' thread leak that the 60s keepAlive disproves. v4 nonetheless edges ahead on raw net because its core-pool-churn point matches the sole human reference comment and v5 skipped that topic, while v5 spent two of its five points on praise and a self-answered Javadoc note. Both candidates verified the BookKeeperServer default-change impact correctly, and both missed the build-breaking unused static import of getServerMaxThreads that violates the project's checkstyle UnusedImports rule. On judged quality, v5's analysis is the one an engineer should trust, despite the near-tied scores.
